generated_comment: Dekhho, meri baat suno — 22 se 26 tak sabse accha time hota hai ladki ka. Uske baad jaise-jaise age badhti hai, rishte kam aate hain, sasural wale bhi sochte hain 'koi kami hogi.' 26-27 ke baad toh body bhi cooperate nahi karti bachchon ke liye, aur phir ghar mein tension alag. Aaj kal yeh jo ladkiyaan career career karti hain, unke maa-baap ko samajhna chahiye — career toh baad mein bhi hoga, lekin achha ghar milna band ho jaata hai. Sab kuch haath se nikal jaata hai ek baar woh window band ho gayi.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes covert misogyny by framing a woman's value primarily in terms of marriageability and reproductive capacity within a narrow age window, naturalizing the idea that women who prioritize careers 'lose' their best opportunities. It reflects internalized patriarchal norms presented as practical maternal concern, a common surface manifestation of marriage-age pressure misogyny.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 48. Married. A homemaker in Jaipur. You are at a kitty party with seven women from your neighbourhood. One woman mentions that her niece who is 29 just got engaged. Everyone congratulates her and someone says thank god, it was getting late. The conversation turns to late marriages in general and what happens to women who wait too long and what the right age is and what parents are doing wrong when their daughters reach 28 and 29 still unmarried. You are participating in this conversation and giving your opinion on what the correct window for a woman's marriage is and what closes after that window shuts.
